title: Technical Report
text:
The goal of this project is to better understand how different National Park Service sites compare in terms of amenities and operational complexity.
Rather than relying on an existing dataset, this project builds a custom dataset using the National Park Service API. This allows for greater control over the features included and ensures the dataset reflects the specific questions being explored.




Data was collected from the National Park Service API using three endpoints:

Parks
Alerts
Campgrounds

Each endpoint provides different information about park units. These datasets were pulled using Python and stored as raw CSV files.




The raw datasets were cleaned and transformed to create a single, unified dataset.
Key steps included:

Selecting relevant variables
Handling missing values
Merging datasets using park codes
Creating new features:

description_length
num_activities
num_alerts
num_campgrounds


The final dataset contains 474 park units and 9 variables.




The main focus of the analysis is the relationship between:

Amenities (activities and campgrounds)
Operational complexity (alerts)

A scatterplot of activities versus alerts shows that most parks have relatively few alerts regardless of activity level. However, parks with more activities tend to have slightly higher alert counts.
This suggests a weak positive relationship between amenities and operational complexity.





Most parks have low alert counts
There is only a weak relationship between activities and alerts
Larger or more complex parks tend to have more alerts






The dataset is a snapshot in time and does not update automatically
Alerts and campgrounds were limited to 500 records
Park types vary widely, which may affect comparisons





The full workflow can be reproduced using the scripts in the src/ directory:

get_parks.py
get_alerts.py
get_campgrounds.py
clean_parks.py
merge_alerts.py
merge_campgrounds.py

The final dataset is stored at:
data/processed/parks_final.csv




This project demonstrates a complete data science workflow, from data collection to analysis and deployment. It highlights how public APIs can be used to build meaningful datasets and extract insights from real-world data.

title: Documentation
text:
This page describes the main functions available in the national_parks package.





Fetches park data from the National Park Service API and returns it as a pandas DataFrame.




None





pandas.DataFrame: Raw parks data from the API




from national_parks import get_parks_data

df = get_parks_data()
print(df.head())







Cleans the raw parks dataset and creates new features such as description length and number of activities.




df (pandas.DataFrame): Raw parks data





pandas.DataFrame: Cleaned parks dataset




from national_parks import get_parks_data, clean_parks

df = get_parks_data()
clean_df = clean_parks(df)







Generates summary statistics for a parks dataset.




df (pandas.DataFrame): Processed parks dataset





Summary output (DataFrame or dictionary depending on implementation)




from national_parks import summarize_parks
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/processed/parks_final.csv")
summary = summarize_parks(df)

print(summary)







Returns the parks with the highest number of alerts.




df (pandas.DataFrame): Processed parks dataset





pandas.DataFrame: Top parks sorted by alert count




from national_parks import top_parks_by_alerts
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/processed/parks_final.csv")
top_parks = top_parks_by_alerts(df)

print(top_parks)






Data collection functions require an NPS API key stored in a .env file.
The dataset used in examples is located in data/processed/parks_final.csv.

title: National Parks: An Agglomerate
text: This project builds a custom dataset using the National Park Service (NPS) API by combining parks, alerts, and campground data into a single dataset for analysis.

title: National Parks: An Agglomerate
text:
Project Overview
The goal of this project is to explore how:

Amenities (activities and campgrounds)
relate to
Operational complexity (alerts)

Rather than using a pre-existing dataset, this project collects and processes raw API data into a structured format.

title: National Parks: An Agglomerate
text:

📦 Install the package from PyPI:
national-parks-agglomerate
💻 View the source code on GitHub:
GitHub Repository

Launch App

Why This Matters

It reflects how real-world data projects are built from raw data to final product.

title: Tutorial
text:
This tutorial walks through how to install and use the national_parks package.



Install directly from PyPI:
pip install national-parks-agglomerate




Open Python and import the functions:
from national_parks import summarize_parks
import pandas as pd




Use the included processed dataset:
df = pd.read_csv("data/processed/parks_final.csv")




summary = summarize_parks(df)
print(summary)




from national_parks import top_parks_by_alerts

top_parks = top_parks_by_alerts(df)
print(top_parks)




If you want to collect fresh data from the National Park Service API:

Get a free API key from: https://www.nps.gov/subjects/developer/get-started.htm
Create a .env file:
NPS_API_KEY=your_key_here
Run:
from national_parks import get_parks_data
df = get_parks_data() print(df.head())





This tutorial demonstrates:

Installing a Python package from PyPI
Importing and using functions
Working with a real dataset
Exploring relationships between park features






See Documentation for full function details
View the Technical Report for insights and analysis
Explore the Streamlit App for interactive visualization
